text:
Great Britain's elite are up in arms over the "ridiculous"Paris 2024 Olympicsrule forcing full-priced tickets even for their babychildren.

With a sizableBritishcontingent of 327 athletes heading to Paris 2024, many are parents with infants eager to witness their sporting prowess.Yet, according to Paris 2024 officials, every spectator, regardless of age, must secure a paid-for seatinfants included.

Outrageously, the event organisers proclaimed: "All spectators will need a validgetLocation ticket to access an Olympic venue, including children of all ages. The same rates will apply."

Gold medal hopeful and British track cyclist Dan Bigham, whose one year old son Theo is affected by this policy, vented: "It seems so illogical that I have got to buy a ticket for a one-year-old."

Mockingly he added toThe Sun:"Officially he takes a seat, even though he won't be able to sit on it at a year old. It's annoying. Is it worth £300? He won't remember it. But it will be nice to have him there."

Echoing his frustration, British rower Mathilda Hodgkins-Bryne, who plans to bring her two year old son Freddie to Paris, fumed: "It is absolutely ridiculous." She added: "Any human being, even if they are in a sling, they have to have a ticket."

Indignant at having to shell out for her offspring previously allowed in free due to his age, she argued: "I have never had to pay for rowing tickets for Freddie before given his age. We know that with the noise of the Games, he is literally going to come into my race and leave, but we have still got to pay for that."

Competitors are allocated two match tickets per session they participate in with a catchthey're not gratis.

Hodgkins-Byrne, 29, expressed her frustration: "By having to pay for little ones, who are not even going to sit there and enjoy it, it means people like our parents can't go."

She added the emotional toll, saying: "It's a bit of a shame. But I will be significantly less stressed and less upset if Freddie is there than if he wasn't."

Furthermore, the ticketing terms highlight that "access to the venues is not recommended for children below the age of four". This policy caught some fans off guard, especially those who did not anticipate parenthood at the time of their ticket purchase.

It is noted that London 2012 faced similar concerns but later reversed their stance, allowing infants under 12 months into venues without a ticket.

title: Team GB fury as ‘ridiculous’ Paris Olympics rule for babies costs £300 | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Paige Ingram, Ellie Grant
description: A number of Team GB's 327-strong delegation are parents and will have their children with them in the French capital for the 2024 Paris Olympics
